stories.title: Legal Holds in Investigations: eDiscovery Best Practices
stories.author: Doug Austin
stories.summary: A comprehensive guide on implementing effective legal holds during investigations, covering preservation obligations, custodian identification, hold notices, and compliance monitoring. Essential reading for legal teams managing investigation-related eDiscovery.
key_points: Legal holds must be issued promptly when litigation or investigation is reasonably anticipated
Custodian identification should be broad initially and refined as facts develop
Hold notices should be clear, specific, and acknowledge receipt
Regular compliance monitoring and documentation is critical
Failure to implement proper holds can result in sanctions and adverse inference instructions
stories.content: When an organization faces an investigation – whether regulatory, internal, or civil litigation – one of the first and most critical steps is implementing a legal hold. This article covers best practices for legal holds in investigation scenarios, including preservation obligations, custodian identification, hold notice drafting, and compliance monitoring. The article emphasizes that legal holds must be issued promptly when litigation or investigation is reasonably anticipated, and that custodian identification should be broad initially and refined as facts develop. Hold notices should be clear, specific, and require acknowledgment of receipt. Regular compliance monitoring and thorough documentation are critical throughout the process. Failure to implement proper holds can result in severe consequences including sanctions and adverse inference instructions.

stories.title: Case Where Plaintiff Tried to Use ChatGPT in a Deposition is Dismissed
stories.author: Doug Austin
stories.summary: A significant case where a plaintiff attempted to use ChatGPT during a deposition, resulting in case dismissal. This ruling highlights the boundaries of AI use in legal proceedings and the importance of procedural integrity.
key_points: Plaintiff attempted to use ChatGPT to assist during a live deposition
Court found this violated procedural rules and deposition protocols
Case was dismissed as a result of the improper AI usage
Ruling establishes important precedent for AI use in legal proceedings
Emphasizes need for clear policies on AI tools in litigation
stories.content: This case law analysis examines a significant ruling where a plaintiff attempted to use ChatGPT during a deposition. The court found this violated procedural rules and deposition protocols, ultimately dismissing the case as a result of the improper AI usage. This ruling establishes an important precedent regarding the use of AI tools in legal proceedings, emphasizing the need for clear policies on AI use in litigation contexts. The case serves as a warning about the boundaries of AI assistance in formal legal proceedings and highlights the importance of procedural integrity in depositions.

stories.title: Microsoft Launches Word Legal Agent for Contract Review
stories.author: Legal Technology
stories.summary: Microsoft has launched a new Word Legal Agent, integrating AI-powered contract review directly into Microsoft Word. This move positions Microsoft as a direct competitor to specialized legal technology vendors.
key_points: Microsoft launches Word Legal Agent with built-in contract review capabilities
Integrates AI analysis directly into the familiar Word environment
Positions Microsoft as competitor to specialized legal tech vendors
Leverages Microsoft's massive user base for rapid adoption
Could significantly disrupt the legal technology market
stories.content: Microsoft has entered the legal AI arena with the launch of Word Legal Agent, a new feature that brings AI-powered contract review capabilities directly into Microsoft Word. This move positions Microsoft as a direct competitor to specialized legal technology vendors who have traditionally dominated this space. By integrating AI analysis into the familiar Word environment, Microsoft leverages its massive user base for potential rapid adoption. The feature represents a significant expansion of Microsoft's AI capabilities into the legal vertical, and could substantially disrupt the legal technology market as users may prefer the convenience of an integrated solution over separate specialized tools.

stories.title: Definely Launches MCP, Warns Against AI Self-Checking
stories.author: Legal Technology
stories.summary: Definely has launched a Model Context Protocol (MCP) enabling lawyers to call Definely's review tools from within enterprise AI environments. The company emphasizes that asking AI to check itself should not be a solution for legal work.
key_points: Definely launches Model Context Protocol (MCP) for enterprise AI integration
Enables calling Definely's structured review tools from AI environments
Key message: 'Don't ask AI to check itself' - need deterministic verification
Troutman Pepper Locke already deploying alongside their Athena AI agent
Provides auditable, repeatable results vs. generative model self-review
stories.content: Definely – the contract technology company used by law firms and in-house teams including A&O Shearman, Slaughter and May, Troutman Pepper Locke, Samsung and KPMG – has launched a Model Context Protocol (MCP), enabling lawyers to call Definely's specialist review tools from directly inside their enterprise AI environments. As the volume of AI-generated drafting threatens to outstrip the speed of human review, expert judgement has never been more critical, and asking an AI to check itself should not be a solution for clients, risk committees and courts. Definely says that its MCP closes this gap by equipping lawyers with an objective, deterministic baseline for review. Definely treats contracts as structured objects. The MCP uses Definely's tools to deliver repeatable results and the output is auditable in a way that a generative model's review of its own work cannot be. 'Generative AI can benefit from tools offering structured analysis, much like the way they might use a calculator tool for maths,' said Shima Rashidi, head of AI at Definely. 'The Definely MCP connects Enterprise AI platforms to our structured review tools, allowing the AI to report verifiable facts, providing the repeatable and auditable results that human accountability demands in the legal sector.' Troutman Pepper Locke, which has deployed Definely alongside its proprietary Enterprise AI agent Athena, welcomed the MCP launch.

stories.title: eDiscovery Market Shift: Collection Growing 4x Faster Than Review by 2030
stories.author: ComplexDiscovery Staff
stories.summary: Major market analysis reveals a significant shift in eDiscovery spending: collection is projected to grow at ~16% CAGR through 2030, four times faster than review. Review's share of total spend drops from 62% to 52% as AI compresses per-document costs.
key_points: eDiscovery market: $19.61B (2025) to $28.08B (2030)
Collection: $3.33B to $7.02B at ~16% CAGR - fastest growing segment
Review share drops from 62% to 52% of total task spend
AI-assisted review compressing costs faster than data growth offsets
Vendor selection shifting toward forensic depth and modern collection capabilities
stories.content: The worldwide eDiscovery market spent approximately $19.61 billion across the three core tasks of collection, processing, and review in 2025. Six in every ten of those dollars went to review. By 2030, the reconciled view places review's share at five in every ten – even as absolute review spending continues to rise. Collection's share over the same five years climbs from 17 percent to 25 percent. The composition shift inside the market reveals a story the aggregate growth line does not: AI is compressing per-document review costs faster than data growth offsets them, while collection – exposed to data-volume expansion but largely insulated from AI-driven cost compression – pulls dollars toward itself at premium rates. In 2025, the reconciled view places worldwide review spending at approximately $12.16 billion, processing at approximately $4.12 billion, and collection at approximately $3.33 billion. Collection expands at a compound annual rate approaching 16 percent through 2030—roughly four times the growth rate of review. Three observations follow for professionals: First, vendor selection criteria continue to shift away from review labor capacity and toward forensic depth and modern collection capabilities. Second, pricing pressure in managed review not only persists but accelerates as AI-assisted review becomes the default rather than an optional enhancement. Third, the discoverable surface area continues to expand into domains—such as AI prompt logs, ephemeral messaging, and structured operational systems—where automation has not yet caught up with the work.
